steps.dialogue: You've learned comparing fractions with the same denominator. Now let's figure out why this works and see how it connects to other math ideas.
Notice what happens here. All these fractions are fifths. How do we know we can order these fractions by just looking at the denominators?

options: All the denominators are the same
The numerators count up by one

step.dialogue: What do all these fractions have in common at the bottom?
Look at the denominators, the fractions are [event:698a20146112b4b3]all fifths. Since the denominator is 5 for all the fractions, we can compare by numerators.

metadata.description: Highlight all denominators, then show counting sequence, then select the correct answer

step.dialogue: Here's the key: [event:698a2a108d61b1c6]all of these fractions have the same denominator of 5. When denominators match, the pieces are the same size. So [event:698d28fe4b008433]we can order by counting: [event:698a2a69bb5b78d3]1 piece, [event:698a2abf6dad4350]2 pieces, [event:698a2ace09512033]3 pieces, and so on.

on_correct.dialogue: Exactly. Same [event:698a206c59dd0642]denominators mean same-size pieces. When pieces are the same size, we can order fractions [event:698d29421e5de618]by comparing numerators, [event:698a20968636b930]just like ordering whole numbers. [event:698a21147715dab5]This works for fractions with ANY denominator, thirds, fifths, elevenths, as long as denominators all match.

steps.dialogue: Our comparison method works in real life too. Think about this scenario: Alex and Sam are both reading the same book that has 12 chapters. These bars show us how much they've each read. Who has read more chapters?

options: Alex read more because [fraction numerator=5 denominator=12]5/12[/fraction] > [fraction numerator=8 denominator=12]8/12[/fraction]
Sam read more because [fraction numerator=8 denominator=12]8/12[/fraction] > [fraction numerator=5 denominator=12]5/12[/fraction]
They read the same amount because       [fraction numerator=5 denominator=12]5/12[/fraction] = [fraction numerator=8 denominator=12]8/12[/fraction]

step.dialogue: Let's check again. We can compare [fraction numerator=5 denominator=12]five twelfths[/fraction] to [fraction numerator=8 denominator=12]eight twelfths[/fraction]. Who read more?

metadata.description: Highlight 5 parts on Alex's bar, then 8 parts on Sam's bar, then select the correct answer

step.dialogue: Let me help you: Alex read [event:698a31773b7450d7][fraction numerator=5 denominator=12]five twelfths[/fraction] of the book. Sam read [event:698a3188247f080e][fraction numerator=8 denominator=12]eight twelfths[/fraction] of the book. [event:698a31e7792eb737]Since 8 is greater than 5, [event:698a31a0d726c528]Sam read more chapters.

on_correct.dialogue: Right. Sam read [event:698a3354144fa9e6][fraction numerator=8 denominator=12]eight twelfths[/fraction] of the book, more than [event:698a3365f4d82a04]Alex's [fraction numerator=5 denominator=12]five twelfths[/fraction]. That's how people solve these problems in real life.

steps.dialogue: Let's look at another problem. Which is bigger, [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction] or [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction]? What makes this problem different? Why don’t the strategies you learned previously work here?

prompt.text: Select ALL the reasons why our strategies don’t work.

options: There are the same number of pieces

step.dialogue: You're on the right track. Take another look at the denominators and the pieces in each bar.
Think about how these ideas connect: different [event:698a3b83aeb075b7]denominators lead to different [event:698a3ba9f70da3d6]piece sizes, which leads to needing a different method.

metadata.description: Highlight denominators showing they're different, show piece sizes are different, then select all three options

step.dialogue: All three of these ideas connect together. We start with different [event:698a3663efbf9e6b]denominators, thirds and fifths. Different denominators mean differently sized [event:698a370ad8b8774d]pieces, a third is bigger than a fifth. And when pieces are different sizes, we can't just count and compare the number of pieces.

on_correct.dialogue: Right. All three of these ideas connect together. We start with different [event:698a3663efbf9e6b]denominators, thirds and fifths. Different denominators mean differently sized [event:698a370ad8b8774d]pieces, a third is bigger than a fifth. And when pieces are different sizes, we can't just count and compare the number of pieces. [event:698a3847f74f2132]We need a different method for this kind of problem. There are ways to compare these fractions. You'll learn those methods soon.

steps.dialogue: Today, you've learned that you can compare fractions with the same denominator using [event:698a45953ba413eb]greater than, [event:698a460c55df2975]less than, and [event:698a4618ebde4453]equals. You're demonstrating that you understand how to compare. Check that the [event:698a46edd24d9f8f]denominators match, then compare [event:698a47611bcdd68b]the numerators. And you understand why the process works. When we have the same denominators, that means we have the same size pieces.
Next time, you'll learn how to compare fractions even when the denominators are different. You're building a strong foundation for understanding fractions.
